description: In environments with VRRP or VSX peers, while performing mutual route leaking on the VRRP peers with BGP neighborship established in between and towards the upstream network, the switch will install both routes as ECMP instead of preferring the leaked route. Use route-maps to give lower/higher preference to the routes received from an iBGP peer. For example: Copy !

messages.content: For 6300_6300L_6400 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what caveat is documented for BGP in environments with VRRP or VSX peers?

Feature Caveat: BGP - In environments with VRRP or VSX peers, while performing mutual route leaking on the VRRP peers with BGP neighborship established in between and towards the upstream network, the switch will install both routes as ECMP instead of preferring the leaked route. Use route-maps to give lower/higher preference to the routes received from an iBGP peer.

description: Symptom: Intermittent MSTP/RPVST starvation messages observed coming from source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00. Scenario: When 3rd party legacy devices send packets with source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00 to CX devices intermittent BPDU starvation might be observed. description: Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.

description: Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.

Workaround: Avoid continuous adding or removing VRF and restart system daemon hpe-routing.

description: Symptom: ops-switchd crash while collecting VXLAN diagdump or collecting support files all. Scenario: In some VXLAN scenarios with multiple LAGs and multiple overlay VLAN on those lags. After device reboot, the LAG interface could show incorrect VXLAN Programming. If the user attempts to remove VLANs, VNI-to-VXLAN configurations that belong to the affected LAGS, a ops-switchd crash might be observed. Workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.

description: Symptom: Delay of ~30s observed when updating VLAN access config via snmpset and verifying via snmpget immediately after. Scenario: When snmpset changes one OID and snmpget reads another OID before the cache can result in not updated cache values for OID. Workaround: Use the CLI for VLAN access configuration instead of SNMP.

description: Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

scenario: 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected

Scenario: This issue can occur while changing port speeds to 2.5G on a 6300 switch using gPTP. Workaround: Use 1G/5G/10G port speeds instead.

description: Symptom: TFTP Software image upload/download transfer operation fails. Scenario: Downloading/uploading the software Image via sm ubuntu IPv6 TFTP server fails. description: Symptom: Traffic is dropped for a few seconds on a MACsec channel during a VSF switchover. Scenario: When the MACsec channel has data-delay protection enabled, there can be traffic drops for a few seconds on the channel post a VSF switchover due the reset of the MKA session on the interface. Workaround: Do not use data-delay protection in a MACsec policy if the system is deployed as a VSF stack.

description: Symptom: When modifying DHCP Server pool parameters (such as DNS server entries) using Aruba Central template-based configuration, the changes are accepted without warning but do not take effect. The running DHCP configuration continues using the previous values. Scenario: Configuration updates to a DHCP Server pool require the DHCP Server to be disabled and re-enabled (or the switch rebooted) before the new settings become active. When these same changes are pushed through Central templates, they do not take effect. Workaround: For CX software versions prior to 10.15.1020, after making any DHCP server pool configuration change, perform one of the following to activate the new configuration Disable and re-enable the DHCP server instance, or Restart the DHCP server process, or Reboot the switch.

description: Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.

description: Symptom: The switch doesnt learn the MAC of a client that is denied on a secure port even when its moved to a trusted/non-secure port. description: Symptom: ops-switchd crash while collecting VXLAN diagdump or collecting support files all. Scenario: In some VXLAN scenarios with multiple LAGs and multiple overlay VLAN on those lags. After device reboot, the LAG interface could show incorrect VXLAN Programming. If the user attempts to remove VLANs, VNI-to-VXLAN configurations that belong to the affected LAGS, a ops-switchd crash might be observed. Workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.

description: Symptom: When modifying DHCP Server pool parameters (such as DNS server entries) using Aruba Central template-based configuration, the changes are accepted without warning but do not take effect. The running DHCP configuration continues using the previous values. Scenario: Configuration updates to a DHCP Server pool require the DHCP Server to be disabled and re-enabled (or the switch rebooted) before the new settings become active. When these same changes are pushed through Central templates, they do not take effect. Workaround: For CX software versions prior to 10.15.1020, after making any DHCP server pool configuration change, perform one of the following to activate the new configuration Disable and re-enable the DHCP server instance, or Restart the DHCP server process, or Reboot the switch.
